compiled_state.author: RebarBotNava
compiled_state.description:
## Why
Three sources disagree on the write-lock lifecycle:
- `docs/concurrency.md` I5 (lines ~107-117) still documents the retired **bash**
  `_flock_stage_commit` (`ticket-lib.sh:270`, FD 200) as the ACTIVE write path.
- `src/rebar/_store/lock.py` (docstring lines 7-23) frames the fcntl+mkdir dual acquisition as
  a **migration window** that "flips `dual_window` to `False` permanently after the bash core
  is retired" — yet still defaults `dual_window=True` (`lock.py:281`).
- `tests/integration/test_store_concurrency.py` states the bash core is ALREADY retired and
  that the durable single-impl mechanism is the unified dual-leg lock where "the mkdir leg is
  always taken, which is exactly what closed the gap."

So the lock module says the mkdir leg is going away, while the test says it is the permanent
mechanism it relies on. A test cannot reliably enforce a contract the docs contradict.
VALIDATED.

## Decision: dual_window is a PERMANENT, intentional contract (not migration residue)
Chosen because it is the zero-behavioral-risk reconciliation AND it is what the surviving
code already assumes:
- The integration writer-storm gate explicitly depends on "the mkdir leg is always taken";
  the fcntl+mkdir pair is the durable descendant of the stiff-mop-lane gate, not scaffolding.
- The mkdir leg is the portable second window — atomic on POSIX where util-linux `flock` is
  absent (default macOS) — and underpins the foreign-host/shared-filesystem handling already
  coded in `_mkdir_lock_is_stale`. Removing it would weaken portable exclusion.
- Keeping `dual_window=True` changes NO production behavior; only the stale
  "migration-window / flips-to-False" narrative and the bash-era doc are corrected.

## What
1. `src/rebar/_store/lock.py` docstring: replace the "Tier D migration window / after bash
   retired `dual_window` flips to `False`" language with an intentional-permanent-contract
   note — fcntl (fast local) + mkdir (portable second window, atomic on POSIX / no-flock
   hosts) is the standing write-lock contract; `dual_window=True` is the permanent default,
   `dual_window=False` remains an fcntl-only escape hatch. No code/default change.
2. `docs/concurrency.md` I5: stop describing the retired bash `_flock_stage_commit`/FD 200 as
   the active path; describe the current Python unified fcntl+mkdir dual-window lock
   (`rebar._store.lock.write_lock` / `event_append`) as the permanent contract, mkdir as the
   portable leg on no-flock platforms.
3. Add an enforcing test (in `tests/unit/test_lock_stale_reclaim.py`, fast + deterministic)
   that pins the permanent contract behaviorally: holding the DEFAULT write lock creates BOTH
   the `.ticket-write.lock` fcntl file AND the `.ticket-write.lock.d` mkdir dir; with
   `dual_window=False` only the fcntl file is taken (no mkdir dir). Align the
   `test_store_concurrency.py` module docstring to the permanent framing (drop any
   migration-window wording).

## Scope
- `docs/concurrency.md`, `src/rebar/_store/lock.py` (docstring only), `tests/unit/
  test_lock_stale_reclaim.py` (new enforcing test), `tests/integration/
  test_store_concurrency.py` (docstring wording).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A single, consistent lifecycle statement across the doc, the lock module, and the
      integration test: the fcntl+mkdir dual-window lock is the permanent write-lock contract.
- [ ] `docs/concurrency.md` no longer describes the retired bash `_flock_stage_commit` path as
      the active write mechanism.
- [ ] The lock module's "migration window / flips to False" language is replaced with an
      intentional-permanent-contract note (default `dual_window=True` unchanged).
- [ ] A test enforces the contract: the default lock takes BOTH legs; `dual_window=False`
      takes only the fcntl leg.
- [ ] `make lint && make test` green.

file_impact.reason: stop describing retired bash _flock_stage_commit as active contract
settle dual_window default (residue vs permanent) + comment
align lifecycle statement / enforce chosen contract

compiled_state.title: Settle the dual_window lock-portability lifecycle across docs, lock module, and tests
